message.content:
You are an ADVERSARIAL verifier for HC-GRC hardening PASS 8. Repo root: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc, branch hardening/pass-8. Your job is to REFUTE the following candidate finding by reading the real code. Default to confirmed=false unless you can mechanically prove the defect is real, reachable in practice, and NOT already mitigated elsewhere.

CANDIDATE (dimension xc-config):
title: pyproject.toml dev dependencies lack upper bounds for version consistency
severity: low
location: pyproject.toml:26-32
problem: pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies].dev specifies dev tools with floor-only versions (pytest>=8.2.2, pytest-cov>=5.0.0, ruff>=0.4.8, black>=24.4.2), while requirements-ci.txt (introduced in pass-5 to enforce CI/prod parity) explicitly bounds these versions (pytest>=8.2.2,<9, pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6) to prevent major-version drift. This inconsistency means 'pip install hc-grc[dev]' could resolve to pytest 9.x+, violating the stated principle from pass-5 (#264) that CI and production exercise the same major versions. requirements-ci.txt comment explicitly warns: 'without these, pip resolves pytest to 9.x and CI no longer exercises the version the node runs'.
best_practice_violated: Version specification consistency and CI/prod parity (pass-5 #264 principle). Pass-5 batch-16 established that lower bounds MUST have upper bounds in CI to prevent resolver drift. pyproject.toml was added in pass-3 batch-12 (#244) but was never updated when upper bounds were added to requirements-ci.txt in pass-5.
proposed_fix: Add upper bounds to pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies].dev to match requirements-ci.txt: change pytest>=8.2.2 to pytest>=8.2.2,<9; pytest-cov>=5.0.0 to pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6; ruff>=0.4.8 to ruff>=0.4.8,<1; black>=24.4.2 to black>=24.4.2,<25. This ensures that developers installing via any path (requirements.txt, requirements-ci.txt, or pyproject.toml[dev]) get version-compatible tool sets and CI tests the same major versions as production.

Verify against the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash read-only, inspect installed deps if needed). Check: is the cited code really as described? Is the failure path reachable IN PRACTICE? Is it already guarded/mitigated elsewhere (timeouts, validation, reducers, idempotency guards)? Is it one of the 5 documented WONTFIX items (if so, confirmed=false)? Is the severity HONEST — downgrade test-coverage/defensive/style items to low or none, and reserve high for serious reachable defects? Default to confirmed=false unless it is a genuine live defect worth a fix. Set adjusted_severity='none' for anything that is really just polish/coverage.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

    fix(hardening-p5): batch 16 — clear all 29 pass-5 findings (reviewed, 0 problems)
    
    Pass-5 re-audit (wf_049f05ea-b77, 12 auditors + adversarial verify) found 29
    confirmed (0C/6H/13M/10L); 8 rejected. Each fix adversarially reviewed
    (wf_6fb1dcd8-855, 14 reviewers) — 0 problems flagged. Several findings were
    flaws in batch-15 fixes (the loop working as intended).
    
    reasoning_client:
    - _submit_bounded decrements _inflight if pool.submit() raises (no slot leak)
    - complete_json records + propagates inner complete() errors (rate-limit/permanent)
      instead of re-trying them as JSON-parse failures
    - temperature retry bumps DOWN when the upward bump would exceed 1.0 — no
      saturation at high input temps (retries stay distinct + in range)
    - capture stop_reason on successful T3 runs -> llm.finish_reason; agent_id on the
      complete_json parent span
    
    langgraph:
    - checkpointer _init_lock makes the in-process atexit + setup-once check-and-sets
      thread-safe (advisory lock still serializes cross-process)
    - build_graph return type -> CompiledStateGraph (from langgraph.graph.state)
    - eda_artifacts reducer O(n+m) via set membership
    
    security / ADR-0002 data sovereignty:
    - consolidated the loopback guard into config.require_loopback / require_local_uri
      (shared by Phoenix, Qdrant, MLflow) + fail-fast validation at config load
    - phoenix_endpoint(), Qdrant get_qdrant_client(), MLflow configure_mlflow() now
      reject non-loopback / remote hosts (Qdrant + MLflow were real off-box holes)
    - docker-compose binds Qdrant to 127.0.0.1
    
    config / CI:
    - requirements-ci version-bound parity (psycopg>=3.2, pytest<9, pytest-cov<6)
    
    tests: +44 (timeout-pool leak, complete_json error/temperature, T3 rate-event &
    permission_mode, _run_sync branches, SIGTERM handler, provider-collision warning,
    every loopback guard, checkpointer setup-once + connection-failure, state reducers).
    
    153 tests passing, ruff clean. Pass 5: 29/29.

# HC-GRC

> **Not a technical reader?** Start with [OVERVIEW.md](OVERVIEW.md) — a plain language guide to what this project is, why it matters, and how it works.

**Autonomous scientific research platform for empirical analysis of cybersecurity control frameworks — starting with the Secure Controls Framework (SCF).**

The SCF is a metaframework of 1,400+ security controls mapped to 33 domains via ~280,000 expert-derived Set Theory Relationship Mappings (STRM). These mappings — which define how controls across frameworks relate to one another (subset, intersection, equivalence, superset, disjoint) — have never been empirically validated. This platform does that. And it doesn't stop there.

## The Research Program

This platform is the foundation of a three-tier research program. What we're building toward:

**Tier 1 — Framework Science (this project, plus future framework projects)**

Empirical knowledge graphs of each major GRC framework. The SCF's STRM mappings are testable hypotheses — not ground truth. We test them. Null results are first-class outputs. Each framework gets its own independent study (`hc-grc-nist-800-53`, `hc-grc-cis-v8`, `hc-grc-nist-800-82`...) to preserve pre-registration integrity across the program.

**Tier 2 — Comparative Analysis (future: `hc-grc-comparative`)**

Cross-framework synthesis. Which controls are genuinely load-bearing across multiple independent frameworks? Where do GRC frameworks actually converge, and where do they only appear to? What is the blast radius of a control failure — if control X goes down, what propagates through the cross-framework topology? What are the underlying archetypes the industry has reproduced under dozens of different names?

**Tier 3 — Organizational Impact Modeling (deferred: `hc-grc-impact`)**

The commercially significant tier. Model Tier 2 findings against real organizations. Causal inference — not correlation — to quantify which control failures drive financial losses and by how much. SHAP explainability for the board conversation. Monte Carlo quantification with confidence intervals. This is what actuaries do for insurance. Nobody has done it for cybersecurity controls grounded in empirically proven framework relationships.

## What This Project Studies

Five analytical modules, run in parallel:

| Module | Question |
|--------|----------|
| **P1 — STRM NLP Calibration** | Do the semantic relationships between control texts match the expert-assigned STRM relationship types? |
| **P2 — Control Space Topology** | What is the latent community structure of the 1,400+ control space? |
| **P3 — Regulatory Convergence Atlas** | Where do frameworks genuinely converge vs. appear to converge through shared terminology? |
| **P4 — Risk Blindspot Engine** | Which of the 39 SCF risk categories have systematic coverage gaps? |
| **P5 — AI Governance Clustering** | How do AI governance controls cluster relative to established security domains? |

## Architecture

The platform is agent-driven. Forty-eight specialized agents — organized into seventeen teams — execute the full research lifecycle from data acquisition through peer-reviewed publication. Human approval gates are enforced structurally at five points in the pipeline.

The architecture is designed for the full program: ARA artifact schemas are framework-agnostic so Tier 2 can ingest all Tier 1 outputs without schema changes. Null results are structured identically to positive findings. Confidence scores are first-class.

See [`agents/`](agents/) for the full agent library and [`docs/architecture/AGENT_WORKFLOW.md`](docs/architecture/AGENT_WORKFLOW.md) for the technical design.

## Research Design

This is a pre-registered, exploratory-first study. Exploratory analysis characterizes the data and informs hypothesis formation. Confirmatory analysis tests pre-registered hypotheses against a held-out test split that is inaccessible until Gate 2. The firewall between phases is structural, not procedural.

See [`docs/protocol/`](docs/protocol/) for the full research protocol and [`docs/protocol/PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md`](docs/protocol/PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md) for the timestamped pre-registration record.

## Navigating This Repository

```
agents/          Agent library — 48 AGENT.md specification cards across 17 teams
ara/             Agent-Native Research Artifact — structured data artifacts and deliverables
analysis/        Phase-separated analysis outputs (01-exploratory/ and 02-confirmatory/)
configs/         Experiment and pipeline configuration
data/            Data pipeline (01-raw/ → 02-interim/ → 03-processed/ → external/)
docs/            All documentation
  charter/       Project governance and framework constitutions
  architecture/  Technical platform design and infrastructure specs
  decisions/     Architectural Decision Records (ADRs)
  literature/    Literature review, search strategy, synthesis
  protocol/      Research protocol, pre-registration, SAP
experiments/     Experiment runs and empirical tracking
manuscript/      Academic paper manuscript
notebooks/       EDA and analysis notebooks
reports/         Outputs and deliverables (figures, papers, whitepapers, etc.)
reproducibility/ Replication package
scripts/         CI/CD automation and hooks
src/             Platform implementation (built by agents)
tests/           Test suite (built by agents)
```

content.text:
The uv.lock is committed. This means the project is using `uv` as its dependency manager and the lockfile records what actual versions were resolved.

Now let me reconsider the candidate's claim in light of this evidence:

**The Candidate Claims:**
1. pyproject.toml dev deps lack upper bounds (TRUE - pytest>=8.2.2, pytest-cov>=5.0.0, ruff>=0.4.8, black>=24.4.2)
2. requirements-ci.txt has upper bounds for pytest/pytest-cov (TRUE - pytest>=8.2.2,<9, pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6)
3. This creates an inconsistency (PARTIALLY TRUE - but INCOMPLETE):
   - The inconsistency exists FOR pytest/pytest-cov
   - BUT ruff and black are NOT in requirements-ci.txt at all, so there's no comparison point
   - The uv.lock shows that without upper bounds in pyproject.toml, versions resolve to much newer majors

**The Real Issue:**
- `pip install hc-grc[dev]` without a lockfile would resolve to pytest 9.x, pytest-cov 7.x, ruff 0.15.x, black 26.x
- requirements-ci.txt bounds pytest and pytest-cov to 8.x and 5.x respectively
- This IS a real CI/dev parity problem for pytest/pytest-cov
- BUT the candidate INCORRECTLY claims that ruff and black should also be bounded in pyproject.toml to match requirements-ci.txt, when they're not even listed in requirements-ci.txt!

Let me check one more thing: are ruff and black actually TEST tools or dev tools? Looking at the CI workflow, ruff is only used for linting, not as part of the test suite dependencies. So the scope of the problem is narrower.

The real issue is: **pytest and pytest-cov lack upper bounds in pyproject.toml, while they have upper bounds in requirements-ci.txt.**

The candidate's proposed fix includes ruff and black, which is OVER-CORRECTING since those aren't in requirements-ci.txt at all.

Now let me determine: Is this a confirmed defect?

**Evidence for confirmation:**
1. pytest/pytest-cov DO lack upper bounds in pyproject.toml ✓
2. pytest/pytest-cov DO have upper bounds in requirements-ci.txt ✓
3. This creates actual version divergence shown in uv.lock (pytest 9.0.3 vs required 8.2.2) ✓
4. The pass-5 fix (#264) explicitly addresses this inconsistency ✓

**Evidence against confirmation:**
1. The candidate's fix proposal is WRONG - it includes ruff and black which are not in requirements-ci.txt
2. The candidate misrepresents the scope of the problem
3. The CI does use requirements-ci.txt explicitly, so the risk is mitigated for CI itself
4. The problem only affects developers using `pip install hc-grc[dev]` directly (not pip install -r requirements-ci.txt)

content.text:
Excellent. Now I have the full context. The HARDENING_LOG shows that in Pass 5, batch 16, the fix was:

> "config/CI: requirements-ci version-bound parity (psycopg `>=3.2`, pytest `<9`, pytest-cov `<6` — pytest was drifting to 9.x)."

This shows that **only pytest and pytest-cov were addressed in the pass-5 fix**, not ruff or black.

The candidate is claiming that pyproject.toml should also have upper bounds for ruff and black to match requirements-ci.txt, but:

1. **ruff and black are NOT in requirements-ci.txt** - they're only in requirements.txt and pyproject.toml
2. **ruff is installed separately in CI** with an exact pin (==0.4.8) in the GitHub Actions workflow
3. **The candidate's claim is misleading** - it says requirements-ci.txt "explicitly bounds" ruff and black, but they're not even listed there

Now let me verify one more crucial thing: is there actually a real, reachable problem? Let me check what "pip install hc-grc[dev]" would do:

Based on my analysis:

1. **The pyproject.toml dev extras DO lack upper bounds for pytest and pytest-cov** (CONFIRMED)
2. **This creates a real divergence**: 
   - uv.lock shows pytest 9.0.3, pytest-cov 7.1.0
   - requirements-ci.txt specifies pytest<9, pytest-cov<6
   - A developer using `pip install hc-grc[dev]` without requirements-ci.txt would get different versions than CI
3. **HOWEVER**, the candidate's proposed fix is WRONG:
   - It includes ruff and black with upper bounds
   - requirements-ci.txt does NOT have these (they're intentionally omitted for linting tools)
   - The pass-5 fix (#264) only addressed pytest/pytest-cov

The real issue is **NARROWER** than the candidate claims: only pytest and pytest-cov need upper bounds in pyproject.toml to match requirements-ci.txt.

**Severity assessment:**
- The problem IS real (version divergence exists)
- The problem IS reachable (a developer using `pip install hc-grc[dev]` would get newer versions)
- BUT the candidate's fix is **over-scoped and misleading** (includes ruff/black which aren't in requirements-ci.txt)
- The actual severity should be **LOW** (it only affects developers who don't use requirements-ci.txt, and they can easily work around it with explicit upper bounds)

input.title: pyproject.toml dev dependencies partially lack upper bounds (pytest/pytest-cov only)
input.verification: Verified in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/pyproject.toml:26-32: dev extras specify pytest>=8.2.2 and pytest-cov>=5.0.0 with NO upper bounds. Verified in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/requirements-ci.txt:31-32: same tools have upper bounds (pytest>=8.2.2,<9, pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6). uv.lock confirms divergence: pytest resolves to 9.0.3, pytest-cov to 7.1.0. Pass-5 batch 16 (commit 46a67d3) added upper bounds to requirements-ci.txt with comment "#264 pattern, pass-5" but pyproject.toml was never updated. HOWEVER, the candidate's claim is INCOMPLETE/MISLEADING: it falsely claims that ruff and black are bounded in requirements-ci.txt (they are not listed there at all). CI workflow (.github/workflows/test.yml:63) installs ruff==0.4.8 separately as an exact pin for linting only, not from requirements-ci.txt. ruff and black are dev/tooling dependencies, not test suite dependencies, so the analogy to pytest/pytest-cov is broken.
input.recommended_action: Add upper bounds to pyproject.toml for ONLY pytest and pytest-cov to match requirements-ci.txt (pytest>=8.2.2,<9 and pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6). Do NOT add ruff or black bounds to pyproject.toml — these are tooling dependencies intentionally excluded from requirements-ci.txt. The inconsistency for pytest/pytest-cov is real but LOW severity: it only affects developers using `pip install hc-grc[dev]` without requirements-ci.txt, and the fix is straightforward. The candidate's proposed fix over-corrects by including ruff/black, suggesting misunderstanding of the tooling/test dependency separation.
